blocks.heading: Word processing software is used to make documents. 📝
blocks.html:
You can write letters, reports, and stories.

Examples are Microsoft Word and Google Docs.

blocks.heading: A font is the style of your letters. 🔤
blocks.html:
Different fonts look different.

Example: Arial looks plain. This looks fancy.

blocks.heading: Font size changes how big the letters are. 🔍
blocks.html:
Small text is good for long paragraphs.

Big text is good for titles and headings.

blocks.heading: Font style adds a look to your letters. 🖊️
blocks.html:
Bold makes text thick and dark.
Italic makes text slanted.
Underline puts a line under the text.

blocks.heading: Alignment is where your text sits on the page. ↔️
blocks.html: Text can be lined up left, centre, right, or justified (even on both sides).

blocks.heading: Line spacing is the gap between lines of text. 📏
blocks.html:
More space makes a document easier to read.

Less space fits more text on one page.

blocks.heading: You can add tables, pictures, and objects. 🖼️
blocks.html:
A table holds information in rows and columns.

Pictures and objects make your document more interesting.

blocks.heading: A header is text at the top of every page. ⬆️
blocks.html:
A footer is text at the bottom of every page. ⬇️

Headers and footers often show a page number or title.

blocks.title: Match each word to its meaning. 🧩

pairs.definition: Where text sits on the page ↔️
Text at the top of every page ⬆️
Text at the bottom of every page ⬇️

question.explanation: Font size changes how big or small your letters look on the page.
A footer is text that repeats at the bottom of every page, like a page number.

question.text: Which alignment makes text even on both sides? ↔️
question.explanation: Justified alignment spreads text evenly so both the left and right edges line up.

blocks.heading: Formatting is changing how a document looks. 🎨
blocks.html:
Good formatting makes a document easy to read.

It does not change the words, just the look.

blocks.heading: Use bold for titles and important words. 💪
blocks.html:
Bold text stands out from normal text.

Don't bold too much, or nothing stands out.

blocks.heading: A table organises information in a grid. ▦
blocks.html:
It has rows (across) and columns (up and down).

Tables are good for lists, prices, and schedules.

blocks.heading: Tight line spacing fits more on a page. 📐
blocks.html:
Wide line spacing is easier on the eyes.

Reports often use 1.5 or double line spacing.

blocks.heading: Pictures should match your topic. 🖼️
blocks.html:
Resize a picture by dragging its corner.

Keep pictures small enough that text still fits.

blocks.heading: Page numbers go in the header or footer. 🔢
blocks.html:
They help readers find a page quickly.

Most documents number every page automatically.

blocks.heading: Keep formatting consistent. ✅
blocks.html:
Use the same font and size all through your document.

This makes your work look neat and professional.

blocks.title: Put these document formatting steps in the correct order. 📝

question.text: What is a table used for? ▦
options: Organising information in rows and columns
question.explanation: A table organises information into rows and columns, like a list or schedule.

question.text: Why should your formatting stay consistent? ✅
options: It makes the document look neat
question.explanation: Using the same font and style throughout makes your document look neat and professional.

question.text: Where can a page number be added? 🔢
question.explanation: Page numbers are usually placed in the header or footer so they appear on every page.

blocks.heading: Proofreading means checking your work for mistakes. 🔍
blocks.html:
Always proofread before you finish a document.

Mistakes are easier to fix early.

blocks.heading: Spell check finds spelling mistakes. 🔡
blocks.html:
It often shows a red wavy line under wrong words.

Right-click the word to see suggestions.

blocks.heading: Grammar check finds sentence mistakes. ✍️
blocks.html:
It often shows a blue or green wavy line.

It checks things like missing words or wrong tense.

blocks.heading: Spell check does not catch every mistake. ⚠️
blocks.html:
It cannot tell if you used the wrong word correctly spelled.

Example: "their" instead of "there". Always read your work too!

blocks.heading: Print preview shows how your document will look on paper. 🖨️
blocks.html:
Use it before you print to check the layout.

This saves paper and ink.

blocks.heading: Print options let you change how a document prints. ⚙️
blocks.html:
You can choose the number of copies.

You can also choose which pages to print, and colour or black & white.

blocks.heading: Always check before you click print. ✅
blocks.html:
Check spelling, grammar, and the print preview.

This stops you from wasting paper on mistakes.

blocks.title: Match each word to its meaning. 🧩

pairs.definition: Shows how the page will look on paper 🖨️

question.explanation: Spell check looks for words that are spelled wrong, often shown with a red wavy line.

question.text: Why should you still read your own work after spell check? ⚠️
options: Spell check cannot catch every mistake, like the wrong word used correctly spelled
question.explanation: Spell check cannot tell if a correctly spelled word is the wrong word, like "their" instead of "there".

question.text: What does print preview show you? 🖨️
options: How the document will look on paper
question.explanation: Print preview shows exactly how your document will look once it is printed on paper.

blocks.heading: A file is one saved item on your computer. 📄
blocks.html:
A document, photo, or song are all files.

Every file has a name.

blocks.heading: A folder holds files together. 📁
blocks.html:
Folders help you stay organised.

You can put folders inside other folders too.

blocks.heading: Give your files clear, simple names. 🏷️
blocks.html:
A good name tells you what is inside.

Example: English_Essay_Draft1 is better than Document1.

blocks.heading: Naming conventions are rules for naming files. 📋
blocks.html:
Many schools and workplaces use one style.

Example: Subject_Task_Date, like Maths_Test_2026-06-29.

blocks.heading: Version control means tracking changes to a file. 🔄
blocks.html:
Add a version number when you save a new draft.

Example: Essay_v1, Essay_v2, Essay_v3.

blocks.heading: Version control stops you losing your best work. 💾
blocks.html:
If a new version breaks, you can go back to an older one.

Never overwrite your only saved copy without a backup.

blocks.heading: A password protects your files and accounts. 🔒
blocks.html:
Use a password that is hard for others to guess.

Never share your password with people you don't trust.

blocks.heading: A strong password mixes letters, numbers, and symbols. 🔑
blocks.html:
Example: Sunset7!Kite is strong.

Example: 123456 is weak and easy to guess.

blocks.title: Complete this strong file name: Maths_Homework_______
blocks.explanation: Adding a version number like v1 helps you track changes if you save more drafts later, such as v2 or v3.

question.explanation: A folder holds files together so you can stay organised and find them easily.

question.text: Why use version control, like naming files v1, v2, v3? 🔄
options: It tracks changes so you can go back to an older version
question.explanation: Version control tracks changes over time. If something goes wrong, you can return to an older saved version.
A strong password mixes letters, numbers, and symbols. This makes it much harder to guess.
